column1: What two steps read an analog resistive sensor with an MCU?;Convert the resistance into a voltage the ADC can sample (a divider)
column2: then convert the digitised voltage back into engineering units in firmware (invert the model).

column1: What is an NTC thermistor?;A Negative Temperature Coefficient resistor — its resistance falls as temperature rises. The relationship is strongly nonlinear (roughly exponential)
column2: giving high sensitivity but needing model inversion.

column1: What is the Beta (β) equation for an NTC?;R(T) = R0·exp[β(1/T − 1/T0)]
column2: with T in kelvin and R0 the known resistance at reference T0 (usually 25°C = 298.15 K). Rearranged: 1/T = 1/T0 + (1/β)·ln(R/R0) — the form firmware uses to get T from R.

column1: What is the Steinhart-Hart equation?;A more accurate three-coefficient NTC model
column2: 1/T = A + B·ln R + C·(ln R)³. The β equation is enough to understand the principle but Steinhart-Hart is used for higher accuracy.

column1: How does a voltage divider read the NTC?;Put the NTC in series with a fixed resistor Rs across the supply and tap the midpoint. With the NTC on the bottom leg

column2: and why is it robust?;If the ADC's reference is Vcc
column4: so Vcc cancels: R_NTC = Rs·code/(Nmax−code). Supply variation no longer affects the reading — you never even need to know Vcc accurately.

column1: What are the three steps of the NTC firmware chain?;Sample the ADC for code in [0
column4: then recover temperature via the inverted β model.

column1: Why pick Rs near R_NTC at your target temperature?;Divider sensitivity (∂V_out/∂R) is greatest when the two resistances are comparable
column2: so Rs ≈ R0 maximises resolution around the temperature you care about (e.g. room temperature).

column1: What is self-heating in an NTC
column2: and how do you limit it?;Current through the NTC dissipates power (P = V²/R) and warms it
column3: so the sensor reads high. Keep the divider current low (larger resistors)
column4: or sample briefly and power the divider only during conversion.

column1: Why linearise or use a lookup table for an NTC?;The curve is so nonlinear that resolution varies across the range. A lookup table or piecewise linearisation around the region of interest often beats evaluating the full equation
column2: and is mandatory without an FPU (avoids logf).

column1: Why filter ADC readings before inverting the NTC model?;ADC readings are noisy and the nonlinearity amplifies noise unevenly
column2: so average several samples (or run a simple IIR filter) before applying the inverse model.

column1: Simpler fix than a precision reference when a sagging Vcc drifts the NTC reading?;Drive the divider from the same node that is the ADC reference (Vref = Vcc) — i.e. read ratiometrically. Vcc then appears in both the divider output and the reference and cancels
column2: so supply sag no longer matters and no extra reference chip is needed.
